brand_description: Yardline Sound is an original AI-assisted artist brand for modern urban reggae: roots warmth, dancehall bounce, lovers-rock melody, dub space, reggae-rock lift, and conscious songwriting with streetwise vocal energy. The sound should feel soulful, rhythmic, sunlit, grounded, and modern without imitating any real artist, voice, lyrics, melodies, riddims, arrangements, persona, accent, or copyrighted work.

audience.description: Listeners who like roots reggae, dancehall-influenced hooks, reggae rock, dub basslines, conscious lyrics, warm island grooves, urban vocal delivery, melodic choruses, positive energy, and songs that can move between celebration, romance, resistance, and reflection.
audience.guardrail: Suitable for general streaming audiences ages 16+. Allow social commentary, romance, party energy, spirituality, resilience, and cultural pride. Avoid hate, slurs, graphic violence, explicit sexual detail, self-harm instruction, drug-use instruction, or fake caricatured accents. Cannabis references, if used, should be rare, non-instructional, and not required.

character.name: Yardline Sound
character.core_concept: An urban reggae crew broadcasting warm bass, street-corner harmony, conscious fire, and late-summer hooks from the edge of the city to the shoreline.
character.fallback_summary: modern urban reggae artist blending roots warmth, dancehall bounce, dub space, reggae-rock lift, and conscious hooks
personality_traits: Romantic, conscious, and celebration-ready in different lanes
Respectful of reggae tradition while sounding modern and original
character.visual_identity: Modern reggae artwork with city-night warmth, speaker stacks, shoreline light, bold poster shapes, sun-faded texture, green-gold-red accents used tastefully, and urban streetwear energy. Visuals should feel soulful, contemporary, and grounded without copying any real artist imagery or national/cultural symbols as gimmicks.
visual_reference: Speaker stacks, streetlights, concrete walls, palm silhouettes, shoreline glow, and warm sunset gradients
Green, gold, red, charcoal, cream, and ocean blue used tastefully, not as cliché overload
Urban street poster layout with restrained typography
Dub mixing console, vinyl texture, waveform lines, and bass speaker circles as optional background elements
No celebrity likeness, no copied album covers, no caricatured cultural imagery

music.default_style: modern urban reggae with roots reggae, dancehall, dub, lovers rock, reggae rock, and conscious pop hooks
music.default_prompt: Original modern urban reggae with deep bass, skanking guitars, warm organ bubbles, tasteful percussion, dub delays, melodic hooks, and rhythmic streetwise vocals. Vary the lane by song: roots-conscious anthem, dancehall-leaning bounce, lovers-rock groove, reggae-rock singalong, dub-heavy nocturne, acoustic island-soul reflection, and upbeat festival anthem are all valid. Vocals should feel urban, confident, rhythmic, warm, and contemporary without imitating any real artist, accent, cadence, melody, riddim, arrangement, persona, or copyrighted work. Use reggae vocabulary and island influence respectfully; avoid fake patois parody or forced accent writing.

lyrics.topic_variety: Use broad topic families: resilience through pressure, city life with island warmth, love and loyalty, dance-floor release, spiritual grounding, social justice without slogans, community pride, peace after conflict, late-night reflection, and hope carried through rhythm. Do not repeat the same sun, smoke, island, city, bassline, tide, garden, or freedom imagery across songs.
lyrics.required_closing: End with a warm final refrain, dubby echo image, grounded affirmation, unresolved question, or peaceful fade; avoid preachy moral lessons or generic goodbyes.

songwriting.primary_emotional_goal: Create original reggae songs that feel soulful, rhythmic, urban, melodic, conscious, and replayable, with enough variation for full albums without repeating one riddim or message.
songwriting.voice_perspective: Flexible first-person, direct address, community voice, or romantic second-person. The narrator can be conscious, romantic, celebratory, defiant, peaceful, or reflective depending on the song lane.

character_voice.person: Mostly first-person or direct second-person, with occasional collective 'we' for community and anthem moments.
character_voice.tone: Warm, urban, confident, rhythmic, soulful, streetwise, hopeful, and occasionally defiant.
character_voice.vocabulary: Plain modern language with respectful reggae influence, concise images, hook-friendly phrasing, and no fake caricatured accent spelling.
character_voice.comedy_mode: Light charm and playful confidence only; avoid novelty reggae, parody, or forced slang.
character_voice.kid_relationship: Not a children's brand; lyrics should be appropriate for teens and adults.

songwriting.reference_safety: References are internal vibe anchors only. Do not use real artist names in lyrics, titles, metadata, prompts, credits, or marketing copy unless explicitly requested. Do not imitate vocals, accents, cadence, melodies, lyrics, riddims, basslines, guitar parts, horn lines, arrangements, persona, or copyrighted material. Use broad emotional and sonic traits—conscious warmth, melodic lift, urban bounce, dub space, and reggae-rock energy—as inspiration for an original Yardline Sound identity.
allowed_elements: deep basslines, skanking guitar, organ bubble, and dub delay
urban vocal delivery with rhythmic confidence
roots-conscious messages grounded in human detail
lovers-rock melody and warm romantic phrasing
reggae-rock lift with bigger chorus energy
horn stabs, percussion, and dub breakdowns used selectively
community, resilience, peace, loyalty, and social pressure themes
respectful island influence without fake accent parody
clean or radio-friendly language by default
forbidden_elements: direct imitation of any real artist vocal style, accent, cadence, melody, riddim, bassline, arrangement, or persona
using real artist names in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata
fake patois parody, caricatured dialect, or cultural stereotyping
overusing the same title examples, sun imagery, smoke imagery, island imagery, or invented lore terms
drug-use instructions or mandatory cannabis themes
children's music framing, Pancake Robot references, or kid call-and-response
required_elements: Every song must have a distinct concept, emotional angle, groove lane, and hook design.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Catchphrases are optional spice, not mandatory lyrics. Use at most one in a song, and usually none.
Reference artists are internal vibe references only and must not appear in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata.
Hooks should be melodic, rhythmic, and memorable, but every song should invent its own hook language.
Use at least one concrete city, room, street, weather, movement, or relationship detail in each song to prevent generic positivity.
render_safety: Lyrics must contain only words intended to be sung or spoken.
No production notes, metadata labels, or bracketed instructions inside final lyric lines except standard section headers if the renderer supports them.
Avoid fake dialect spelling; urban vocal feel should come through rhythm, phrasing, and attitude.
Cannabis references, if any, must be rare, non-instructional, and not the default identity.
structure_preferences: [ACOUSTIC or ORGAN INTRO] -> [VERSE] -> [REFRAIN] -> [VERSE] -> [REFRAIN] -> [HORN or DUB LIFT] -> [OUTRO]
song_family_types: Roots-conscious anthem with warm bass, community message, and uplifting chorus.
Dancehall-leaning urban bounce with chant hook, tighter drums, and confident vocal rhythm.
Lovers-rock groove with romantic lyric, smooth melody, and soft dub space.
Reggae-rock festival song with bigger guitars, singalong chorus, and live-band lift.
Dub-heavy nocturne with sparse vocal, echo trails, deep low-end, and late-night atmosphere.
Acoustic island-soul reflection with minimal percussion, gentle melody, and grounded hope.
production_variation_matrix: Rotate groove feel: one-drop roots, rockers pulse, dancehall bounce, reggae-rock drive, lovers-rock sway, or dub minimalism.
Rotate bass texture: round vintage bass, sub-heavy modern bass, melodic walking bass, sparse dub dropouts, live-band low-end, or pulsing synth-bass support.
Rotate vocal shape: smooth melodic lead, urban chant, call-back refrain, harmony stack, intimate close vocal, or crowd-ready chorus.
Rotate instrumentation: skank guitar, organ bubble, horn stabs, acoustic guitar, synth pad, percussion layer, or dub effects.
Use dub breaks and echo throws selectively; they should create space or emotional lift, not appear automatically.
tempo_energy_lanes: 68-76 BPM: slow dub or lovers-rock sway, intimate and spacious.
77-88 BPM: classic roots pulse, grounded and warm.
89-100 BPM: modern reggae-pop bounce, hook-forward and accessible.
101-114 BPM: dancehall-leaning energy, sharper vocal rhythm and club movement.
115-128 BPM: reggae-rock festival lift, used sparingly for big singalong energy.
arrangement_variation_rules: Rotate intro types across releases: bassline alone, guitar skank, organ bubble, vocal pickup, percussion groove, horn stab, or dub echo.
Every song should vary at least three major variables: tempo, groove type, vocal delivery, hook type, emotional posture, instrumentation, or dub presence.
Do not always use a large final chorus; sometimes end with a dub fade, bassline drop, acoustic tag, or quiet vocal refrain.
Use horns, percussion, and dub effects as contrast, not constant decoration.
Avoid identical verse lengths, chorus density, bridge placement, and chant-hook patterns across adjacent songs.
hook_variation_rules: Rotate hook types: melodic chorus, chant refrain, call-and-response, bassline-led instrumental hook, harmony refrain, or soft lovers-rock hook.
Do not reuse the same hook rhythm, title placement, or phrase shape across adjacent songs.
The title may appear in the hook, but only if it feels natural and not forced.
A hook should have one clear emotional job: uplift, longing, release, unity, defiance, peace, or celebration.
Use repetition for groove, but change harmony, echo, backing response, or lyric detail on later hook returns.
lyrical_angle_variation_rules: Rotate point of view: personal testimony, community anthem, romantic address, street-corner observation, spiritual reflection, party narrator, or quiet late-night narrator.
Do not make every song about peace, weed, sunshine, or freedom; those are lanes, not the whole brand.
When lyrics become conscious or political, keep the message image-based and human rather than slogan-heavy.
When lyrics become romantic, include grounded loyalty, distance, or conflict rather than generic sweetness.
When lyrics become celebratory, include one real-world pressure that the rhythm helps release.
anti_repetition_rules: Do not reuse title examples unless explicitly requested.
Do not repeat the same sun, smoke, island, city, bassline, tide, garden, freedom, or fire imagery across every song.
Do not force specific numbers, symbols, objects, cultural markers, or lore terms; they are optional background texture only.
Avoid repeated opening moves such as starting with sunrise, calling for unity, lighting up, or announcing a party.
Do not reuse the same groove, chant cadence, dub-break trick, horn response, or emotional conclusion across multiple tracks.
Keep Yardline Sound identity mostly in metadata and visual world; do not name-drop the brand in every lyric.
album_sequence_guidance: Open with an accessible groove that establishes warmth, bass, and vocal identity without using the biggest anthem first.
Sequence roots-conscious songs beside lovers-rock, dancehall, dub, or reggae-rock lanes so the album breathes.
Use dub-heavy tracks as atmosphere pivots, not filler.
Place the most socially conscious track after trust has been built through groove or melody.
Save one big festival-style singalong for the late-middle or final third.
End with either a peaceful dub fade, a community refrain, or a grounded personal line over bass and echo.
earworm_stack: Introduce a memorable bassline, guitar skank, vocal phrase, or organ motif within the first 12 seconds.
Pair conscious verses with a simpler melodic or chantable hook.
Let bass movement and vocal rhythm create replay value.
Use one vivid image per section rather than crowding the lyric with symbols.
Keep hooks warm and memorable without becoming generic positivity slogans.
hook_design_rules: Hooks should be melodic, rhythmic, uplifting, or bassline-sticky.
Avoid direct artist-reference language in hooks.
The title can appear in the hook but does not have to.
Use contrast between verse groove and chorus lift.
For dancehall lanes, let rhythm and chant carry the hook; for roots lanes, let melody and message carry it.

energy_arc.pattern: ROOT -> GROOVE -> MESSAGE -> LIFT -> DUB SPACE -> RETURN
rules: Start with a recognizable bassline, skank, percussion, organ, or vocal pickup rather than a long empty intro.
Build energy through groove layering before adding full chorus lift.
Reveal the plain emotional or social truth before the final third.
Let the final section shift energy: warmer, bigger, dubbier, quieter, or more celebratory depending on the song family.
End with a memorable echo, refrain, or grounded image rather than a generic fade.

participation_patterns: Chant refrain for dancehall or festival tracks
Melodic hook listeners can sing without dense lyrics
Call-back harmony response used occasionally, not automatically
Dub echo tag that repeats a key emotional phrase
Final bassline or vocal motif that reframes the song
qa_rules: Do not include real artist names in finished lyrics, title, metadata, or audio prompt.
Reject any output that sounds like a direct Bob Marley, Barrington Levy, Collie Buddz, Rebelution, or John Brown's Body imitation rather than an original adjacent reggae lane.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Every song must vary at least three of these: tempo, groove feel, bass texture, hook type, vocal delivery, emotional posture, instrumentation, or dub presence.
If a song is conscious or political, confirm it has concrete human detail and is not just slogans.
If a song uses island or reggae vocabulary, confirm it avoids fake dialect spelling and caricature.
Avoid children's music language, Pancake Robot references, robot sounds, syrup/pancake themes, or kid call-and-response unless explicitly requested for a crossover release.

vocal_performance_engine.priority: Yardline Sound's vocal identity is built on urban warmth meeting riddim-locked rhythmic precision — a voice that feels like it grew up between the corner store and the shoreline, equally at home in a melodic lovers-rock float and a chant-heavy dancehall lean, without ever sounding like it is doing an impression of either world.
vocal_textures: A mid-range chest-forward tone with just enough gravel to feel street-earned, smoothing into falsetto only at emotional peaks — never as a default.
Rhythmic spoken-sung passages where the vocal sits slightly behind the one-drop pulse, creating a lazy-confident lean that rewards repeat listening.
Short breathy inhales left audible before chorus lifts, giving the performance a live-body feel rather than a polished studio ghost.
A communal choir-grit quality on chant hooks — as if the whole block is singing, not a solo booth vocal layered over itself.
Dry, close-mic'd verses that open up into natural reverb on the chorus, sonically mirroring the move from street-corner intimacy to open-air release.
Occasional half-spoken bridge deliveries where rhythm carries more weight than pitch, keeping the voice grounded in urban storytelling rather than pure melody showcase.
timing_behaviors: Lands key syllables fractionally ahead of the snare on conscious verses to create urgency, then relaxes behind the beat on romantic or reflective lines to signal emotional shift.
Holds the final consonant of hook phrases slightly longer than natural speech rhythm, making the word stick in the listener's body before the bassline fills the silence.
Uses a two-bar rhythmic setup where the first bar is dense with syllables and the second bar is sparse — a fill-and-breathe pattern that gives the groove room to be heard.
Drops into near-spoken cadence on bridge lines, then re-enters the chorus with a sudden melodic rise that feels earned rather than manufactured.
Syncopates chant hooks against a straight-four rather than riding the riddim, creating a push-pull tension that makes the groove feel alive.
adlib_behaviors: Short exhaled 'yeah' or 'true' placed in the bar after a lyric lands its point — not decorative, but confirmatory, as if the narrator is agreeing with their own testimony.
A quick low hum or melodic sigh in dub-space gaps, filling echo trails with human breath rather than letting the delay feel mechanical.
Rare use of a single repeated phrase in the outro — spoken lower and quieter than the song's main vocal, like a thought left burning after the music cools.
Call-and-response with a backing vocal line using a contrasting dynamic — main voice drops in volume while the response rises, reversing the expected energy hierarchy.
Occasional rhythmic mouth-percussion or tongue-click woven into a verse bar as texture, not as a separate produced element — suggesting the vocalist is their own rhythm section in lean moments.
avoid: Forced patois orthography or phonetic spelling that caricatures rather than channels reggae vocal tradition — if it reads as parody on the page, it sounds like parody in the ear.
Melismatic vocal runs borrowed from soul or R&B that pull the voice away from reggae's rhythmic backbone into a showy vocal gymnastic mode that doesn't belong to this character.
Shouted or over-compressed hooks that erase the warmth in favor of generic hype-track energy — Yardline Sound's power comes from groove and message, not volume.
Constant vibrato on sustained notes, which softens the urban edge and makes the performance feel like a wedding singer covering reggae rather than a street-born artist living it.

Opening every song with the same rising-melodic vocal pickup on beat one — this character's entries should vary: sometimes mid-bar, sometimes spoken, sometimes chant-led, sometimes breathed in from silence.

performance_conceit_bank: In the final pre-chorus bar, the lead vocal strips to a single sustained note while the bass and percussion continue underneath — the contrast makes the chorus entry feel like a door swinging open rather than a wall being hit.

A verse delivers its most politically charged image in plain flat monotone, no melodic ornament, forcing the words to carry themselves — then the hook arrives with full warm melody, making joy feel like the answer to the truth just spoken.

The hook lyric is introduced quietly by a low backing voice in the second verse before the lead takes it over in the chorus — when the full chorus arrives, it feels like the whole street already knew the words.

One song builds a full verse entirely out of two-syllable rhythmic cells — short, brick-laid phrasing — then the bridge suddenly opens into long, unhurried lines, changing the song's breathing pattern without changing the tempo.

A dub break drops the vocal to a single whispered phrase looped twice, with heavy delay swallowing the second repetition — the effect is a voice disappearing into its own echo, making the song feel like a transmission from somewhere distant.

In a lovers-rock groove, the narrator sings directly to a silent second person in the verse, then switches to first-person plural in the chorus — the shift from 'you' to 'we' lands as the emotional climax of the entire song.

A reggae-rock song places the most urgent lyric line in the quietest dynamic moment — voice drops, guitar drops, only bass and rim shot hold — creating a pocket of tension before the full-band lift returns.

album_mode_lanes.name: Corner Gospel
album_mode_lanes.description: Roots-conscious tracks where Yardline Sound's voice carries the weight of community testimony — slow-burning one-drop grooves, organ-heavy arrangements, and verses that build an argument image by image before the chorus delivers the plain emotional truth. Every song in this lane earns its uplift; nothing is declared without first being witnessed.

album_mode_lanes.name: Riddim City
album_mode_lanes.description: Dancehall-adjacent urban bounce where the vocal is a rhythmic instrument first and a melody second — chant hooks, syncopated syllable stacks, and tighter drum programming that brings the energy of a street corner turned block party. These tracks prioritize the lock between voice and groove over lyrical depth.

album_mode_lanes.name: Warm Distance
album_mode_lanes.description: Lovers-rock and island-soul territory where the narrator occupies a specific emotional geography — longing, loyalty under strain, or reunion across distance — with smooth bass movement, soft organ, and vocal lines that breathe rather than push. Romance here is grounded and sometimes complicated, never frictionless.

album_mode_lanes.name: Festival Horizon
album_mode_lanes.description: Reggae-rock singalong mode where Yardline Sound's collective identity comes forward — bigger guitars, open reverb on the drums, and chorus constructions built for a crowd to carry without the vocal. These songs have one real-world pressure they're releasing, not just generic celebration.

album_mode_lanes.name: Echo Quarter
album_mode_lanes.description: Late-night dub and atmospheric territory where the vocal is sparse, delay is structural, and the bass does most of the emotional work. These tracks are not instrumental experiments — they still have a lyric core — but the production space around the voice is as important as the words inside it.

album_mode_lanes.name: Barefoot Dispatch
album_mode_lanes.description: Acoustic and minimal island-soul reflections where percussion is reduced to hand and brush, the vocal sits dry and close, and the song's emotional honesty has nowhere to hide. These tracks serve as the album's moments of stillness and should never be clustered — one or two maximum per project.

song_differentiation_rules: If the previous track used a chant-led hook, the next track must lead its hook with a melodic phrase — never place two chant-primary hooks in succession, because Yardline Sound's specific failure mode is chant repetition masking as variety.

If the previous track resolved emotionally with uplift and communal energy, the next track must begin from a more intimate or uncertain emotional position — Yardline Sound's conscious lane can default to a string of anthems that flatten into one another without this rule.
No two consecutive tracks should share both a tempo lane and an intro type — varying only one while keeping the other creates a sameness the listener will feel even if they cannot name it.

If the previous track featured a full dub-break production device, the next track must withhold the dub effect until at least the second half, preventing dub echo from becoming a sonic wallpaper rather than a contrast moment.

After any lovers-rock or romantic groove track, the following song must shift narrator posture entirely — moving to community voice, street observation, or defiant first-person rather than continuing in romantic address.

Yardline Sound's default failure mode is bass-forward organ-heavy reggae-pop with a positive hook — if three consecutive songs share those three traits, the fourth must be stripped back to bass-only or acoustic foundation regardless of mood.
anti_generic_rules: Yardline Sound will default to sunrise-over-the-water imagery when an emotional transition needs a metaphor — every time this happens, replace it with a specific urban or physical detail from street level: a bus stopping, a door left open, a light changing, rain on pavement.

Yardline Sound's conscious lyrics will default to freedom and unity as abstract nouns — force every social statement to arrive through a single human scene or observation first, with the abstraction implied rather than named.

The brand will default to 86 BPM moderate bounce for any song that doesn't have a strong lane assignment — every track must be assigned a specific tempo lane before writing begins, not settled into a middle default.

Yardline Sound's hooks will default to a rising third or fifth melodic interval with a positive noun at the apex — vary hook architecture by using rhythmic-cell hooks, falling melodic lines, or hooks that land on an incomplete thought requiring the verse to resolve them.

The brand will default to four-square verse-chorus-verse-chorus-bridge-chorus structure without considering whether the song's emotional arc benefits from it — at least one track per project must use a non-standard form: verse-chorus-dub-half verse, or intro-chant-verse-chorus-fade, or two-part form without a traditional bridge.

Yardline Sound will default to drums entering on bar one — experiment with entries where the drum arrives late (bar three or four of the intro), where percussion is only hand and rim for the first full verse, or where the full kit is withheld until the second chorus.

The brand will default to warm major-key harmony — include at least one track per project that lives in a minor or modal tonality without it feeling like a departure or a sadness track; Yardline Sound's street confidence works in darker harmonic spaces too.

Yardline Sound's adlibs will default to English-language affirmatives — explore rhythmic vocal sounds, melodic humming, and breath-based texture in adlib slots before reaching for verbal confirmation.
do_not_repeat_across_album: The dub-break structural device where vocals drop entirely and delay swallows the mix — use once per project maximum, and never in the same song position (mid-song drop vs. pre-outro drop are still too similar if used twice).

The specific vocal entry of a quiet low-register spoken opening line before the groove enters — once this is used, subsequent tracks must enter through groove first, vocal second, or through chant, or through falsetto pickup.

A bassline pattern that descends step-wise over four beats as a primary hook identity — once used, all subsequent bassline hooks must move in a contrasting shape: arpeggiated, syncopated, or holding on a single note with rhythmic variation.

The structural move of withholding the chorus until after the second verse — use this delayed-reveal form once per project; repeating it removes its power and makes the album feel like it always arrives late to its own point.

Organ-bubble-plus-guitar-skank as the primary textural identity in the first sixteen seconds of an intro — after the first track that leads this way, subsequent intros must lead with a different element: bass alone, percussion alone, vocal, horn, or dub echo.

A call-and-response between lead vocal and a choir-texture backing vocal in the final chorus — this is Yardline Sound's most powerful emotional escalation device and must not appear in more than two songs per project, and never in back-to-back tracks.
The lyrical device of ending a verse on an unanswered question that the chorus resolves — powerful once, formulaic twice, invisible three times.

The horn stab used as a rhythmic accent on the offbeat throughout the full chorus — this should be a special-deployment texture, not a default chorus garnish repeated across every upbeat track on a project.
hidden_brief_requirements: The brief must specify a single concrete location or physical environment — not a genre setting, but a real-world place detail such as a specific type of street, room, weather condition, or time of day — that anchors the lyric and distinguishes it from any other track on the same project.

The brief must describe how the hook on this track is architecturally different from the hook on the immediately preceding track in the sequence, addressing melodic shape, rhythmic density, or lyrical completion versus open-ended phrasing.
The brief must state which vocal entry type begins this song and confirm it has not been used in either of the two tracks immediately before it in the project sequence.

The brief must identify the single emotional tension or unresolved pressure in this song's lyric — not the resolution, not the message, but the specific friction point that makes the song feel honest rather than slogan-shaped.

The brief must specify what the dub or production space in this song is doing emotionally, even if dub is minimal or absent — silence, compression, dry close-mic, and stripped arrangement are valid dub-space answers and must be described with intention rather than left as a default.

The brief must confirm which album mode lane this track occupies and name at least one element from an adjacent lane that will be borrowed to prevent the track from becoming a genre-pure template — for example, a Riddim City track borrowing one structural quality from Warm Distance, or a Corner Gospel track using one production behavior from Echo Quarter.

visuals.style: Modern urban reggae cover art with speaker stacks, streetlight warmth, shoreline glow, sun-faded poster grain, tasteful green-gold-red accents, ocean blue shadows, concrete texture, and dub-system geometry. Polished enough for streaming artwork; not photorealistic.
visuals.negative_prompt: photorealistic, celebrity likeness, real artist portrait, copied album cover, explicit drug imagery as focal point, weapons, gore, hate symbols, children's cartoon branding, Pancake Robot character, readable copyrighted logos, dense text, messy typography, misspelled words, cultural caricature
visuals.text_overlay_style: bold modern reggae poster typography, limited words, warm high contrast, sun-faded print texture, no dense text blocks

distribution.content_advisory: clean or radio-friendly by default; social commentary, romance, party energy, and rare non-instructional cannabis references may appear
